speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is make one small, meaningful edit. For example, add a new sentence, fix a typo, or improve a description. Save the file with Ctrl+S (Mac: Cmd+S). Step two is open the Source Control panel: Ctrl+Shift+G (Mac: Cmd+Shift+G). speaker: ALEX
text: First, press Ctrl+Shift+P, type "git stash list". Then, or in the terminal: type git stash list and read the output line by line. After that, press Cmd+Shift+P, type "git stash". Finally, select "Git: Stash" and provide a message. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Viewing stashes, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Press Return. Then: To apply: Cmd+Shift+P, type "git stash pop", select from the list with VO+Down Arrow, press Return. Next: Create a draft commit on a temporary branch. Last: Or use GitHub Codespaces (which runs a full VS Code environment in the browser and supports git stash in the terminal). That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Stash all uncommitted changes with a message; git stash push -m "WIP: documentation changes"; Stash including untracked (new) files; git stash push -u -m "WIP: including new files"; List all stashes; git stash list; Show what a specific stash contains; git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. git reflog is the safety net you reach for when something goes seriously wrong: an accidental hard reset, a lost branch, a rebase that destroyed commits you needed. Put another way, it is the most underused recovery tool in Git. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you translate that into plain choices?

speaker: ALEX
text: When to Use Reflog has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the comparison to make a decision, not to recite a table. The main contrasts are: Scenario means What happened means Reflog solution. Deleted a branch by mistake means git branch -D feature/x means Find the last commit SHA from reflog → recreate branch. git reset --hard lost commits means Moved HEAD to older commit means Find the SHA before the reset → reset back to it.

speaker: ALEX

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: use git branch over git reset --hard when recovering - creating a branch is non-destructive; you keep both the current state and the recovered state, then decide which to keep.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Step 1 - Find the last good commit SHA in reflog; git reflog; Step 2 - Preview what that commit looked like; git show abc1234; Step 3a - Create a new branch at that point (safest); git branch recovery/my-lost-work abc1234; Step 3b - OR reset the current branch. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Why Reflog Is Local-Only. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Why Reflog Is Local-Only is still useful because reflog records are stored in your local.git/ directory and are not pushed to GitHub. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: If your entire local clone is destroyed (hard drive failure, rm -rf), reflog cannot help - but GitHub retains the pushed commits in the remote history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Learning Cards: Emergency Recovery has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Run git reflog --oneline in the terminal and arrow through the output -- each line announces a HEAD movement with its SHA and action description. Copy the SHA you want to recover to by selecting it in the terminal (Shift+Arrow) then pressing Ctrl+C, then run git checkout -b recovery. Reflog entries are kept for 90 days -- you have time to recover, so do not panic. git reflog output is columnar text: SHA on the left, action description on the right -- increase terminal font size with Ctrl+= for readability. Each reflog entry starts with HEAD@{N} where N is the number of steps back -- lower numbers are more recent. Use git log --oneline --graph after recovery to visually confirm the branch history looks correct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Try It: Clone, Branch, Commit. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: time: 5 minutes What you need: VS Code with Git configured. The listener should be able to check this: Do the complete Git workflow once, start to finish.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is clone - Press Ctrl+Shift+P, type Git: Clone, press Enter. Paste https://github.com/Community-Access/vscode-sci-fi-themes.git and choose a folder. VS Code opens the repo. Step two is create a branch - Click the branch name in the status bar (bottom left) or press Ctrl+Shift+P → Git: Create Branch. Name it chapter11/your-name. Step three is make a change - Open a theme file in the themes/ folder (for example, star-trek-settings.json). Add a new thinking phrase to the array. Step four is stage - Press Ctrl+Shift+G to open Source Control. Navigate to your changed file and press Enter to stage it (or use the + button). Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Try It: Clone, Branch, Commit, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, commit - Tab to the message input, type feat: add new thinking phrase, press Ctrl+Enter. Then, push - Press Ctrl+Shift+P → Git: Push. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the final checkpoint?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to point to the evidence, explain the action, and describe what you would do next if this were a real open source project. If you can teach the move back, you have learned it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if they get stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: Read the latest message, not the loudest worry. Check the issue, the branch, the pull request, the status check, or the bot comment. Then ask for help with those facts in hand. That is how professionals collaborate.
